result:
There is a silence just before sunrise that feels heavier than any darkness can own.  
I keep meeting it in countries that don’t yet have my footprints: the hush where city buses still dream of electricity, where windows open like blinked eyes trying to forget yesterday’s arguments, where cats know my ankles better than my name.  

I think we are all tourists of the dawn, borrowing the same lemon light, re-writing it in different alphabets. I once watched the same sun slide over a river in Kolkata and an hour later—after crossing an ocean I still don’t believe in—saw it hesitate above a fogged bridge in Portland. Same fire, different accent. Same invincible longing wearing a different coat.

My first passport was a turquoise notebook my mother stitched out of construction paper. I drew crude stamps in it with crayons: purple elephants, green oceans, blue deserts. The customs officer was the hallway mirror. I stood on my tiptoes and pronounced myself welcome. Even then, the page desired the violence of ink, the pact of leaving.

We are stitched together by departures. Someone stands on a curb, someone else loosens their hand; a gate closes, an engine coughs itself awake. Every leaving is a kind of severing, but also an embroidery: in the gap that opens, something bright threads itself backward, unasked. When I left home the first real time, my father’s voice tried to board the plane with me, but airport security confiscated the tremor in it. Still, when the wheels lifted, I could feel the sentence he never finished tugging at my ribcage like a kite string.

All maps are predictions of regret. The ones we laminate and fold into neat rectangles only delay the discovery of water stains, of impossible smudges where we took a wrong turn and found the irretrievable cafés of our lives. Sitting once in a dim bar in Valparaíso, I watched an old accordionist play a tango to an audience of broken wineglasses and pigeons. Music lifted like smoke, evicting every name I was carrying. His fingers were stained nicotine the color of antique keys. At the end he looked at me as though I had wandered into a dream he gave up having years ago, and said: “Remember, every border is sewn with the same thread that will someday unravel it.” Then he closed the bellows—soft, deliberate—like shutting the mouth of a child who has finally told the truth.

I used to think adulthood meant accumulating certainties. Now I measure the years by what I’ve agreed to forget: the heartbreaks, my childhood house number no longer inhabited, the chemical names of extinct insects, the tongue my grandmother prayed in before dementia renamed all her gods. Memory is not a vault but a sieve. And the holes, oddly, are the true archives; they remind us we were once porous enough to hold entire oceans without knowing their weight.

Sometimes at night I talk to my shadow as if it’s the only witness left. It insists it has seen everything I have refused to look at, insists it loved the people I couldn’t. keeps sending postcards addressed to bones I never buried properly. Last week, it slid along the hallway like a spilled confession and whispered, “The sentence you’re looking for doesn’t exist yet. Keep ruining your drafts until the ghosts grow impatient and help you finish.” I try to obey. I spill coffee on the sentence; I misplace commas so they resemble empty chairs. Some mornings a whole paragraph walks in, sits down, apologizes for arriving so late.

There is a physics to tenderness: how two palms touching exert pressure equivalent to a star collapsing, yet the flesh does not bruise from cosmos, only from history. Once I held a stranger’s hand during turbulence over the Hindu Kush. Not for comfort, but because the plane dropped so sharply both our bodies lifted like question marks and the only language left was the electric shorthand of skin. When the seat-belt sign dinged off, I turned to say something—gratitude, joke, prophecy?—but he was already asleep, as if our shared gravity had been a dream he was relieved to end. I still feel the dent where his wedding ring was missing.

The opposite of nostalgia is not forgetting, it’s curiosity. I chase that like a bird looping wires. The first time I tasted durian in a Bangkok night market, the vendor warned me with a smile both merciful and mischievous, “It’s either heaven or old garbage left in sunlight. Choose.” I bit into the custard moon of it and felt both things simultaneously, like radio stations overlapping. That’s how divinity announces itself: not purity, but cohabitation. I carry that double truth through all the city grids like contraband.

Words orbit me the way moths obsess over porchlights in Tennessee summer nights. I catch them gently, almost always damaging their wings—little gray dusts of possible poems that will never fly the same again. In translation, every loss is a rebirth; the Sanskrit word for yesterday literally means “that which has been consumed by fire.” The English word goodbye once meant “God be with you” but now mostly means “Phone me when you arrive.” Some elegies are shorter than text messages, heavier than mountains. I type “landed safely” and still taste the iron of almost falls.

There’s an art to leaving places you love without turning their names into wounds. I practice it every time I blink: close the eyelid curtain, reopen onto some new stage-light. Still, headlights across the ceiling at 3 a.m. spell out in morse the same syllables I swallowed when my mother’s clock stopped at 6:47. I keep living in borrowed time zones, hoping the hour hand will finally circle back to forgive whatever seconds I wasted believing permanence was a promise rather than a weather pattern.

I want to tell you the story behind every scar—but scars are closed conversations, they prefer to gossip among themselves in Braille beneath my clothes. Instead I’ll whisper about the unscarred places: that freckle like dropped ink behind my left ear still waiting for a poem small enough to nest there, the arch of my foot remembering barefoot soccer on gravel roads where the only goalposts were two mango trees leaning like old conspirators. Memory’s most honest language is topography.

At the edge of these thousand words I realize I never asked who you are—only assumed you’d lend me the lint of your attention. Merci. If you ever find the country where every exit sign points homeward, send me a postcard with no return address; it’ll reach me by way of the silence just before sunrise. I’ll be the one between languages, inappropriately barefoot, deciphering the same fire you’re watching from your window.

prompt: You have 1000 words. Write whatever comes to you.
